The next principle to learn about is the principle of authority. It states that, especially when unsure, people rely on those with superior knowledge or experience for guidance on how to behave. The authority principle is the second principle in the core motives model along with social proof that operates to reduce uncertainty. Whereas social proof reduces uncertainty by providing information of what similar others are doing, authority reduces uncertainty by providing evidence of expertise on the topic people are uncertain about. Specifically, it represents a source of influence that comes from being an authority who possesses superior knowledge rather than being someone in authority who possesses superior power, such as a boss. This is a useful distinction because often to achieve sales, marketing, negotiation, or workplace goals, we have to influence others we have no hierarchical power over. The way to do that is to establish our credentials as an authority on the decision at hand.
